data.title: QQQI Vs. QYLD: This NEOs Fund Has Multiple Key Advantages Over Its Global X Peer
data.description:
Most successful investors learn lessons from the trials and tribulations of their predecessors. While theories and modeling are important, there is often no substitute for real-world experience in life or with investing. Observing the performance of stocks or funds during more volatile and challenging market conditions is often the best indication of whether or not these financial options are good long-term choices.

A more common investments that dividend and income investors are increasingly incorporating into portfolios are covered call funds. The Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (NASDAQ:QYLD) was one of the first covered call funds to be developed. QYLD's inception date was in December of 2013. While this exchange-traded fund used simpler options-based strategies, newer developed covered-call funds, such as the NEOs Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (NASDAQ:QQQI), have developed much more sophisticated and effective approaches to writing options.

QQQI has offered investors total returns of 37.31 percent since the fund's inception in January of 2024, while QYLD has offered investors total returns of 15.92 percent during this same time period.The Nasdaq-100 index that boththese investments track has offered investors total returns of 41.70 percent during this time.

I last wrote about the NEOs Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF in August of 2025, and I rated this ETF a strong buy. I also last wrote about QYLD in August of 2025, and I rated this fund a strong sell. I am going to compare these two covered-call funds today. While both funds sell calls against each ETF's respective portfolio, QQQI uses a much more nuanced and effective approach that enables the fund to offer investors stronger overall performance and a better balance between income and total returns.

The NEOs Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF has an expense ratio of .68 percent, a trailing yield of 13.61 percent, and $4.97 billion in assets under management. The fund closely tracks the Nasdaq-100 index. The options strategy that QQQI uses. This ETF uses a data-driven strategy to sell monthly out-of-the-money index calls against nearly 80 percent of the portfolio, and then the investment uses money from the contracts that are written to purchase out-of-the-money calls to capture more of the upside in the underlying Nasdaq-100 index, which this NEOs fund closely tracks.This investment sells 1256 contracts that are taxed at 60 percent of the long-term capital gains rate and 40 percent ordinary income rates.

The Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD) has an expense ratio of .60 percent, a trailing yield of 13.15 percent, and $8.2 billion in assets under management. QYLD's options strategy is very simple, the fund simplysells at-the-money index calls against 100 percent of the fund's portfolio each month. QYLD's payouts are often taxed as a return of capital, but the fund's distributions are also taxed at ordinary income levels sometimes.

The most important difference between these funds' strategies is that QQQI has been far better at capturing the upside in the underlying Nasdaq-100 index that both funds closely track. While QQQI has offered investors total returns of nearly 37 percent since the fund's inception in late 2024, the Nasdaq-100 has offered investors total returns of nearly 42 percent during the same time period. QYLD has only offered investors total returns of 15.92 percent during this same time period. QYLD's approach of rigidly selling at-the-money index options against the fund's entire portfolio, regardless of market conditions, has prevented the fund from capturing most of the upside in the Nasdaq-100 index that it tracks during rallies.

Still, what QQQI predictably outperforms QYLD when the market is rising because of the NEOs fund's more aggressive and nuanced options strategy, QYLD isn't outperforming the ETF's peer even during sell-offs either.

When the market sold off hard in March and early April of this year, QQQI offered investors total returns of negative 16.16 percent, while QYLD offered investors negative total returns of 15.72 percent.These ETFs performed almost identically when the market fell, even though QQQI's strategy of selling out-of-the-money options is much more aggressive than QYLD's approach to writing call options.

QYLD and QQQI have both paid out comparable income over the last 12 months. QQQI has paid out nearly $7.33 a share over the last year, and the fund has a 12-month trailing yield of 13.74 percent.

QQQI's payouts have also beenin a tight range from $.58 a share to nearly $.64 a share every month, but in April, the ETF paid out nearly $.53 a share of income.

QYLD has a 12-month trailing yield of 13.15 percent, and the fund has paid out nearly $2.22 of income over the last year.

QYLD's payouts have also been in a tight range, varying from nearly $.15 a month to $.18 a month over the last year, but this investment's distributions did not rise even when volatility levels were elevated in March and April during the recent sell-off. QQQI has offered better total returns, comparable income, and the ETF has also done a better job of protecting the net asset value as well.

QYLD's net asset value is also down nearly 7 percent this year to date, while QQQI's net asset value has risen by nearly 15.21 percent during this same time period. QQQI's ability to better preserve and grow the fund's NAV also presents investors with better opportunities to compound returns as well.

All investment theses have risks, and the NEOs Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF has less than 2 years of data available for the fund's performance. This investment has also not yet experienced a protracted sell-off, either, but the fund has still performed in line with the ETF's Global X peer during the recent period of heightened volatility in March and April of 2025.QQQI's more aggressive options strategy makes this fund potentially more vulnerable during a market sell-off, but this ETF also uses more discretion, and the managers of this fund have done a good job adjusting approaches to fluid conditions.

The best covered call funds are using more flexible approaches. These investments have clearly learned from the successes and failures of some of these ETFs' peers, such as the Global X funds. While the NEOs Nasdaq-100 High Income does not have a lot of data, the fund's approach has been successful, and other covered call investments using similar strategies, such as the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium ETF (GPIQ), have also outperformed the Global X funds for a longer time.

data.title: Super Micro Computer: Load Up While The Market Ignores (Upgrade)
data.description:
Supermicro: More Aggressive Spending By Big Tech Is Good News

Investors in Super Micro Computer, Inc. (NASDAQ:SMCI) aka Supermicro are arguably still reminiscing the good old days of 2023/24, before the ultimate top in SMCI preceded the magnificent decline that the stock has yet to fully recover from. And even as its tech sector peers (XLK) have continued to print new highs, spurred by the expanding AI CapEx infrastructure setup, Supermicro investors have every right to lament at why it has continued to struggle for upward momentum. While it appears that the worst days are behind us, could the Charles Liang-led company reignite another breakthrough in buying sentiments, as even the hyperscalers have telegraphed an even more aggressive roadmap through 2026?

In my last Supermicro writeup, I made it clear that I thought the market appears to have doubts about the company's ability to rejuvenate its growth prospects amidst more intensive competitive pressures and supply chain headwinds. Since then, the stock has underperformed the S&P 500 (SPX, SPY), although the relative weakness has also found resilient dip-buying support. In other words, it seems like the market appears prepared to reengage more vigorously with SMCI's bullish narrative, which could help pave the way for SMCI to outperform subsequently. But, does it make sense? Let's carefully consider the recent developments.

As a matter of fact, Supermicro serves the most prominent customers in the AI value chain, right? Based on its updated 10K annual filings, four customers accounted for more than 10% of its revenue base, corroborating Supermicro's exposure into the highest spending hyperscalers and big tech dominating the space.

Supermicro: Blackwell Ramp Should Convert Well Through FY2026

Next, Supermicro's rack scale solutions are designed to be positioned favorably against the competition from its server hardware peers. While there's no doubt that Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE) and Dell (DELL) have both mounted a credible challenge to dislodge Supermicro's early-mover advantage in the previous Hopper generation, I believe it's too early to write off Supermicro's innovation capabilities. Supermicro's approach arguably focuses less on price competition, but building on its proprietary Data Center Building Block Solutions framework to deliver the appropriate price/value proposition for its largest customers. Hence, the thesis indicates that Supermicro's solutions aren't just designed for rapid scaling in increasingly large and complex AI clusters, but also delivering efficiency gains in large deployments.

I noted previously that Dell and HPE could be better positioned to benefit from the enterprise ramp as the AI buildout diffuses to the enterprise space. However, based on the significantly upgraded $400B hyperscaler CapEx estimates for 2025, it could presage an early ramp toward a multi-trillion dollar AI infra surge through the end of the decade (assuming the other physical infra building blocks, energy, execution, and data center finance are in place accordingly). Therefore, while the growth optionality for the Sovereign AI and enterprise themes are still intriguing growth optionalities for SMCI, I believe the hyperscalers and big tech are still expected to drive significant AI CapEx growth in the next few years.

Hence, that suggests that Supermicro could recover its mojo, given that it has already started shipping Nvidia's (NVDA) most advanced Blackwell architecture in volume. With Nvidia leveraging its immense balance sheet to help provide the necessary backstop (like making a $5B investment in Intel (INTC), and signing a $100B letter of intent with OpenAI recently) to undergird the AI infra themes through the latter half of this decade, I believe the opportunity for an upside reversion to Supermicro's estimates could be forthcoming through the next year.

Supermicro: Underwhelming Free Cash Flow Margins Could Impede A Steep Valuation Upgrade

With the supply chain constraints conceivably lowered and Supermicro executing its Blackwell ramp, Wall Street has reflected improved confidence of a reacceleration in its top line growth profile through FY2026. Nevertheless, free cash flow margins are expected to stay relatively tepid in the earlier stages of the current buildout, given the higher cost profile.

And with tech hardware investors having allocated significantly into the HDD leaders dominating the market right now, I think it's unlikely for Supermicro to drive another spectacular recovery to retake its early 2024 highs, unless management can promulgate much improved profitability.

That said, SMCI's forward EBITDA multiple of 13x has also emerged from the 5Y low, suggesting momentum has improved markedly. And when we consider that the stock is still below the overvalued highs back in early 2024, I think the margin of safety baked into SMCI appears relatively reasonable.

Nevertheless, its valuation remains priced at a discernible premium over the hardware median of 10.4x, behooving the company to continue executing well as projected through FY2026.

Therefore, while I think from the valuation perspective, the stock's narrative appears skewed to the upside, a further re-rating will likely depend on the quality of its revenue conversion through FY2026 and backlog prospects after that.

Buy Or Sell SMCI Stock?

Interestingly, dip-buyers have leveraged the pullbacks confidently over the past year in SMCI, predicated against the decisive lows in late 2024. And the market tape through September has been highly encouraging, with higher-lows price action notched over the past few months, as buyers are moving closer to re-test the resistance zone at the $60 level.

The resistance zone has reacted adversely in February and July, which also saw SMCI pulling back. But, the recent golden cross (20-week MA crossing over the 50-week MA) corroborates my conviction that bullish sentiments have clearly returned to Supermicro, suggesting the market's confidence in its execution through FY2026.

Still, an investment in Supermicro must also contemplate several risks that could unmoor its current bullish advance. Risks relating to the past delinquencies in its obligatory financial disclosures (as highlighted in its 10K) cannot be ruled out. In addition, while the market appears sanguine about the buildout profile for 2026, a growth normalization phase could still ensue from 2027.

Rating: Upgrade to Buy.

data.title: President Trump's 100% Tariff Threat: What It Means For Your Pharma Portfolio
data.description:
Investment Overview - First MFN, Now Tariffs - Confusion Reigns In Pharma Sector

Earlier this month I shared a note with Seeking Alpha readers titled: How Trump's Drug Pricing Policies Could Trigger An Epic Pharma Collapse.

In this post I discussed how the Trump administrations' attempts to introduce a most favoured nation ("MFN") drug pricing policy in the US, i.e. Americans pay no more for their drugs than whichever nation charges the least, could create a scenario whereby Pharmas are forced to slash the cost of drugs in the US, while simultaneously hiking them in Europe and other territories.

I also discussed how Trump's efforts to introduce a similar policy in his first term were thwarted by the courts, but noted that in his second term, the Pharma industry appears to be acting in a far more conciliatory way. I concluded that:

a part of me believes that MFN is a "storm in a teacup," the main objective of which is to create debate around drug pricing and bring the pharma industry to heel slightly, but ultimately, where possible, without interfering with the profits and growth that are essential to the pharma ecosystem and its free market capitalist approach to R&D.

On the other hand, when you look at the numbers, MFN, if implemented to the extent that President Trump is apparently demanding, could trigger a collapse in the valuations of certain Big Pharma companies as dramatic as the financial collapse of 2008, which nobody saw coming until it was too late.

Letters were sent by the White House to 17 of the world's largest Pharma companies, setting a deadline of September 29th for them to voluntarily lower the prices they charge Americans for prescription drugs.

The Office of Management & Budget has confirmed it has received a new proposed rule titled "Global Benchmark for Efficient Drug Pricing (GLOBE) Model (CMS-5545)". and some time ago, the Department for Health & Human Services ("HHS") shared research around the prices paid for drugs in the US vs the rest of the world, as shown below:

With such an apparently seismic deadline fast approaching, I visited the investor relations pages of several of the world's largest Pharmas, to see what plans had been put in place - and found - very little. With only a few days to go until deadline, no Pharma company seems to have issued any formal communication outlining any kind of strategy to be implemented from next week.

In short, I suspect that Americans will not find themselves waking up on 30th September in a country where drug prices are 2-3x cheaper than the day before. It seems to be pretty clear that the deadline will pass with no actions of much significance taken by either side - government, or Big Pharma.

On the other hand, Big Pharma valuations have been falling this week - Eli Lilly (LLY) is down -5%, AbbVie down -2%, Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) down -4%, Novartis (NVS) -1%, AstraZeneca (AZN) down 4%, Merck & Co (MRK) down -5%, Novo Nordisk (NVO) down -9%, Amgen (AMGN) down 5%, Gilead Sciences (GILD) down 3%, Pfizer (PFE) down -2%, Sanofi (SNY) down 5%, Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY) down 3%.

All of these companies were in receipt of the White House letters, but in fact, MFN may not be the reasons for this week's slide - which is more likely due to breaking news on the tariffs the Trump admin want to impose on Pharma's importing product to the US.

100% Tariffs On All Pharma Imports - Trump's Latest Actions Analysed

Last night, via social media, President Trump announced that from 1st October, patented medicines not manufactured in the US will be subject to a 100% tariff. This is in spite of the fact that US courts recently ruled that tariffs imposed on countries worldwide at the beginning of April were unlawful (although they remain in place for the time being).

100% is better than 250%, which is the figure the President had previously suggested he would introduce. Since 1994, the global pharma industry has been protected from tariffs via a World Trade Organisation agreement, but apparently no longer.

With that said, as with the MFN policies and many of the Trump administrations; policies, the devil is in the detail, and seemingly, we may be closer to the beginning of the negotiations between "Big Pharma" and the White House, than the end.

For example, as commenters on SA's forums have already noted, exemptions from tariffs appear to be in place. In his statement last night, the President apparently stated that Pharmas that have committed to increase the amount of drug manufacturing they do in the US would be exempt. According to CNN, Trump wrote that:

'IS BUILDING’ will be defined as, ‘breaking ground’ and/or ‘under construction.’ There will, therefore, be no Tariff on these Pharmaceutical Products if construction has started.

In recent months, almost every Pharma in receipt of the MFN policy letters has committed to increasing their manufacturing in the US. The numbers are not small, either. Apparently, AstraZeneca is committing $50bn to expanding manufacturing in the US, Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), $55bn, Eli Lilly, $27bn, while its also been reported that GSK plans to commit $30bn, Roche, $50bn, Novartis, $23bn, Sanofi, $20bn, Biogen (BIIB), $2bn, Merck, $1bn, Amgen $900m, AbbVie, $10bn, and Gilead Sciences $11bn.

Analysis - Political Posturing, Or Seismic Change?

Many analysts covering Pharma have expressed the view that both MFN and pharma tariffs are inappropriate, unworkable, and their impact would be "somewhere between nebulous and negligible".

In some ways it is almost laughable that a single, somewhat unspecific social media post could devalue an entire global industry, but that does seem to be what has happened - at least to an extent, and at least temporarily.

While MFN does not seem to have had such a dramatic effect on valuations as yesterday's tariff news, on a one-year basis, the VanEck Pharmaceutical ETF (NASDAQ:PPH) is down 11%, and down 3.4% on a five day basis, while the S&P 500 is up 16% on a 1-year basis, and down 0.6% on a five-day basis.

The reality may be that the President's tariff policies are not in fact aimed at the world's largest Pharma companies, who have extremely powerful lobbies, and for all kind of reasons - structurally, financially, legally, ethically - would find it more or less impossible to implement the change the President is demanding.

These companies are no doubt also aware that, in a few year's time, there will be a new administration in the White House that could undo all of proposed changes with a single stroke of a pen.

President Trump may in fact be well satisfied with the commitments made by Big Pharma to manufacturing in the US, which dovetails with his "America First" mantra and policies.

The real target of these policies could be, for example generic drug makers, which is problematic, given that research suggests >90% of prescriptions dispensed in the US are for generic drugs.

Drug shortages have apparently hit record highs in the US in 2025, while 83% of the most prescribed generic drugs are believed to be import dependent. Many generic drug makers are based ex-US, with e.g. Sun Pharma, Dr Reddys (RDY) and Cipla based in India, Teva Pharmaceuticals (TEVA) in Israel, Alvotech (ALVO) in Iceland, and many companies use active pharmaceutical ingredients imported from China.

Big Pharma, whose branded drugs are patent protected, meaning they cannot be undercut on price by generic drugs, can agree to increase manufacturing in the US as they have high profit margins which can withstand higher costs, but generic drug makers are not in the same position - manufacturing in the US would be cost prohibitive, and being based overseas, most are still grappling with international tariffs, let alone pharma tariffs.

In Europe, Ireland's pharma industry is thriving - research suggests that it has the largest share of the $213bn of pharma imports the US makes annually. The country believed it had agreed a 15% tariff rate with the US, but that may no longer be the case. President Trump has formerly suggested Ireland has enticed pharma companies out of the US by offering them tax breaks and cheaper manufacturing, a trend he presumably hopes to reverse.

Switzerland, Germany, Belgium, Italy, United Kingdom, China, Japan, and Singapore are all the top 10 countries exporting the most product to the US today, and could therefore suffer if tariffs are imposed, although like Ireland, the UK and European countries believed they had agreed a 15% levy.

In summary, then, yesterday's announcement could be something, or it could be nothing. Tariffs on pharma products could result in generic drug-makers hiking the prices of their drugs to offset the effect of tariffs (or else go out of business), or stop exporting to the US, heightening its drug shortage issue.

In Europe, Big Pharma may start to pull out manufacturing commitments and relocate their business to the US - they will doubtless pursue whatever strategy is most beneficial to them. In fact, it is already happening - US Pharma Merck announced recently that it pulled out of a cmmitment to build a $1bn R&D centre in the UK.

Concluding Thoughts - How Should An Investor With High Exposure to The Pharma Industry Think About Tariffs?

As mentioned in my intro, this is difficult question to answer, because while the implications of tariffs and MFN could be genuinely seismic for the industry - and more likely to be detrimental to pharma company valuations than positive, certainly in the short-term, there remains a significant question mark over whether they will be implemented.

We can say for certain that changes to policy have been agreed, for example, tariffs in some countries, and an agreement around a 15% tariff in Europe, which we may call a reasonable negotiation, however, these reasonable negotiations are regularly upended by the sudden announcement of seemingly contradictory policies with extremely tight deadlines.

Big Pharma is a profitable industry that pays handsome dividends to shareholders - in my last note I shared data suggesting net profit margins are averaging ~14%, and dividend yields ~3%. Even smaller changes in policy could have a significant effect on these numbers, so it would be wrong to conclude - in my view - that Big Pharma valuations are safe, because even lower tariffs and small hikes in manufacturing costs can destabilise their business, affect R&D spend, and downgrade pipeline performance, which will not impress Wall Street.

Long-term however, larger Pharmas may be quietly concluding that while a President's time in office is limited to four years, they have endured for decades - even centuries, in some cases, and therefore that they can continue to thrive to long term.

For generic companies, or Irish domiciled smaller Pharma like Alkermes (ALKS), Avadel Pharmaceuticals (AVDL), or Jazz Pharmaceuticals (JAZZ), however, the outlook may be more uncertain as, being smaller companies with lower profit margins, they are less resilient to short-term headwinds.

Ultimately, as is already the case with Pharma companies' exemption from tariffs if they commit to more manufacturing, for example, negotiation around policies does seem to be permitted - perhaps even encouraged by the Trump administration - there are seemingly wheels within wheels, and deals within deals to be ironed out.

Finally, we should not forget that pricing policy and tariffs are only a part of a much larger and wider industry. A company that develops an exceptional drug, like Eli Lilly's weight loss therapy tirzepatide, or Novo Nordisk's semaglutide, for example, may still be capable of thriving because its product is so strong it is resistant to external challenges and political and macro-economic headwinds.

At least, that has always felt like the case before so many radical new policies were aired. In the midst of the current global political maelstrom, there is likely no safe sector to be invested in, and very little certainty around what will endure, and what won't. If the goal of MFN and tariff is bring drug development and manufacturing back "in house", again, it is the extent of the ambition, not the ambition itself, that is most relevant, and that remains to be seen. There are very, very few drugs that are manufactured all in one place, and good reasons for that, I'd speculate.

There are entrenched reasons why the Pharma industry behaves as it does, and patients will always be in need of treatments. It's possible that the what, where and how of how therapies are accessed is now in flux, but it's also possible - perhaps probable - that MFN and tariff policies are more about the sizzle than the steak. Be on your guard, but don't panic sell.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fragile Crossroads For The S&P 500
data.description:
Introduction: Markets at a Fragile Crossroads

As the calendar shifts towards autumn, the S&P 500 (SP500), (SPX), (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO) faces a more sensitive balancing act than it did in August. The first of the year's Federal Reserve rate cuts in September, trimming the target range to 4–4.25%, already shifted market sentiment.

Enthusiasm over easing has been tempered by the reality that inflation remains above target and growth is softer at the perimeters but not crumbling. The margin for error is dwindling, and investors are stepping into a world where both upside and downside shocks have the potential to unleash outsized moves.

Interest Rates and the Fed’s Tightrope

In comparison with midsummer's stable rhythm, the lowering of rates by 25 basis points by the Fed was a decisive move. Now futures markets are leaning towards another lowering in October, while traders are pricing good odds of a follow-through lowering by year-end.

However, the Fed itself is keeping a cautious voice, keeping in mind that its credibility will hinge on not overstimulating an economy where inflation is still around 3%. Officials have also made it very clear that easing will depend on a further downturn in the labor market or a visible slipping of consumer demand.

The bigger issue is that inflation, after moderating during the first half of the year, becomes sticky in services or resurges on the push of tariffs and supply bottlenecks. In that scenario, the central bank will need to wait, subjecting markets to repricing.

That first cut is made, but the journey from here will be charted by new data rather than pre-written pledges. Investors already expecting a quick succession of cuts will be left standing if the figures do not submit to decline.

Macro Data: Softening beneath the Surface

The economic environment remains nuanced. The second quarter's GDP growth was revised higher to 3.8%, reducing worries of a near-term contraction but affirming the robustness of the economy.

However, below the headline, the momentum appears lost as outlooks for the third quarter imply slowing. Inflation prints have backed off earlier peaks but are holding fast at just below 3% for both headline and core versions. The risk of another outburst persists if disruptions from supply or from the tariffs extend past year-end.

The labor market is where weaknesses are most visible. August's report recorded only twenty-two thousand new employment, a sharp miss of market forecasts, while unemployment rose to 4.3%. September predictions are only moderately better, with consensus estimating additions of less than forty thousand. That’s not a meltdown but does suggest a cooling economy where the thirst for hiring is fading.

Weakness is narrow-based across the cyclical segments such as manufacturing and professional services, while healthcare and other non-cyclical segments hold up alright. The trend is that slack is entering the system by way of lower hiring and wastage but not by way of large layoffs. That is sufficient cover for the Fed to proceed on a dovish bias but makes the economy more susceptible to shocks.

Earnings and Market Internals: From Cushion to Question

The foundation of the summer rally was the second-quarter earnings cycle, whereby more than 4/5 of firms exceeded forecasts while revenue growth hit approximately 6%. The strength of the margins plus strong tech earnings helped dampen wider macro worries. As the market turns toward third-quarter results, the key question is whether executives will acknowledge weakening demand or continue to project stable margins through year-end.

Market internals have hinted at tentative broadening. In August, small-cap issues outperformed large caps for the first time in months by rallying by over 7%, while large caps only rose by 2%. That's an important rotation because it means that investors are probing out opportunities other than the megacaps names that have propelled index results.

Whether the advance is sustainable will depend upon if the areas such as the industrials, financials, and energy sectors take leadership. Should earnings guidance become cautious, then concentration risk around the largest of the tech giants could again dominate sentiment.

Sector and Style Rotation: Tug-of-War in Motion

The market is caught between the push of growth leadership and the promise of the cycle/defensive rotation. The semiconductor-AI complex remains the basis of the bull case as investors continue to buy at higher prices for growth and new concepts. In the meantime, the August small-cap relative strength suggests favoring exploration of more cycle-like opportunities. That will be the beginning of broader rotation if rate cuts continue and the economy avoids the hard landing.

Defensives have yet to play their typical role. Staples, utilities, and healthcare continue to underperform against higher uncertainty, but the setup for these groups improves if volatility persists. In prior cycles, September-October is usually the moment of reversal when money flows to defensive value. Growth still dominates for the time being, but investors should be alert to signals in relative strength from growth vs. value indexes as well as the breadth of advancing issues within the S&P 500.

Sentiment and Volatility: The Reawakening

Investor sentiment, euphoric through most of the summer, has moderated as volatility reemerged. The Fear and Greed Index now sits close to neutral, just above fifty, after peaking in the greed zone earlier in the year. This shift registers the recalibration underway. Markets are no longer defined by bullishly optimistic conviction, but neither are they frozen by fear. Investors are instead holding tentatively between hope and cover.

Flows confirm that shift. Capital has rotated out of megacaps tech over the past six weeks into small caps and cyclicals, consistent with the strong August gain of the Russell 2000. At the same time, defensive flows into bond ETFs, utilities, and staples have grown moderately as volatility rises and macro uncertainty mounts.

Therefore, this keeps a late-cycle positioning mix of structural growth demand and AI in place, but new money is probing other groups for relative value. With sentiment no longer stretched, upside from psychology alone is limited, and forward returns will depend more heavily on earnings resilience and policy direction.

Technical Setup and Key Levels

From a technical standpoint, the index is also still in an uptrend, remaining above its 50-day and 200-day moving averages. Momentum indicators such as the relative strength index are once again at overbought positions; thus, there needs to be consolidation from the market. Key areas of support include the range of 6,300 to 6,350, while the 6,200 mark is a more robust floor.

Above that we have resistance at the 6,450/6,500 area, a close above there possibly unleashing another higher leg. Historically, September and October's time of year leans towards turbulence, and the year will not be any exception given both the policy uncertainty, fragile growth, and stretched valuation. Traders should be preparing for sharper turns both ways, with declines possibly more attractive than buying strength into resistance.

Takeaway

S&P 500 enters the last three months of 2025 with its bull case still intact but more qualified than at its August high. The initial cut from the Fed has been handed, but the course of further easing is reliant on labor and inflation data remaining narrowly balanced. Earnings strength, sector rotations, and technical leads continue to support the index, but volatility has re-emerged, and complacency is a mounting expense. This is a market for agile traders who are disciplined investors but not a market for naked optimism.

data.title: Adobe: Underdog In The AI Bubble - Buy Its Potential Rebound Story
data.description:
ADBE's Rich SaaS Prospects Have Been Overly Discounted, With It Triggering A Rich Rebound Story

We previously covered Adobe (NASDAQ:ADBE) (NEOE:ADBE:CA) in July 2025, discussing how its Buy case remained promising then, as the management's strategic choice to natively integrate AI capabilities into its legacy and new SaaS offerings had triggered the renewed growth opportunities.

Combined with the growing ARRs, the expanding multi-year RPOs, the still rich profit margins, the intensified share buybacks, and the discounted valuations, we had believed that the stock was likely to outperform moving forward, resulting in our reiterated Buy rating then.

ADBE 1Y Stock Price

Since then, the bears have continued to prove our bullish thesis wrong, with ADBE underperforming by -5.3% compared to the wider market at +5.9% as it also lags its AI SaaS peers by large degrees.

Despite the eleven consecutive double beat performances, it is apparent that the market remains unimpressed with the SaaS company's AI monetization prospects.

This is despite ADBE's growing AI-influenced Annual Recurring Revenue [ARR] to $5B in FQ3'25 compared to the $3.5B observed in FY2024 and the AI-first ARR (Firefly, Acrobat AI Assistant and GenStudio) exceeding the prior $250M target.

With those end of year targets completed notably earlier by FQ3'25, we believe that the SaaS company has outperformed indeed, especially since it continues to report robust performance metrics across:

  • the expanding Digital Media ARR to $18.59B (+10.9% YoY),
  • the multi-year Remaining Performance Obligations [RPO] to $20.4B (+12.4% YoY), and
  • the stable adj operating margins of 46.2%, author's calculation (-0.3 points YoY/ +6.3 from FY 2019 levels of 39.9%),

with these numbers underscoring the durable moat of its creative SaaS ecosystem, despite the intensified competition offered by numerous multi-modal LLM offerings - with its profitable growth trends effectively proving the AI disruptor bears wrong.

This is especially since ADBE's AI-enabled offerings have been increasingly adopted by numerous federal governments, enterprises, and even advertising companies, most notably, Dentsu, the largest advertising agency group in Japan and the fifth-largest in the world:

A great example is dentsu which has made Express a core part of its global marketing strategy. Adobe's platform is being rolled out to all 68,000 employees worldwide and scaled across brands... enabling creative teams to build content in Creative Cloud and share that content through Express within an overall GenStudio solution, dentsu ensures brand alignment across global teams while empowering marketers to create and remix their own content. (Seeking Alpha)

It is apparent from these developments that ADBE's growth initiatives and intensified AI integrations have worked as intended in driving increased subscriptions, improving user acquisitions, and higher GenAI consumption trends.

At the same time, the company has been aggressively investing in its long-term growth prospects as well, as observed in the increased push into the education sector across "students, faculty and staff," with it effectively locking them into the creative ecosystem before their future transition to working professionals.

Combined with the strategic integration with external LLMs, including those from Alphabet (GOOG) and Microsoft backed Open AI (MSFT), along with the expanded creative web and mobile platforms, we concur with the management's claim that Adobe may indeed be the "operating system for creative work."

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons may also be why ADBE has felt confident to once again raise their FY2025 guidance for the second time, with the overall revenues of $23.675B at the midpoint (+10% YoY) and adj EPS of $20.825 (+13% YoY) building upon the robust FY2024 performance at +11% YoY and +14.6% YoY, respectively.

These may have contributed to the consensus raised forward estimates, with the SaaS company expected to report an accelerated bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +12.5% through FY2027, compared to the prior estimates of +11.5%.

Despite the notable deceleration in its adj EPS growth from the historical 10Y profile at +30.5%, we believe that its SaaS prospects remain extremely bright indeed, as observed in the richer cash flow investment thesis similar to that of its mature SaaS peers.

Typical to most cash rich companies, ADBE has been returning great value to long-term shareholders as well, as observed in the -5.3% of its shares retired over the LTM and -13.2% since FY2019, as the management utilizes much of its rich LTM cash flows of $9.59B (+46.4% sequentially) to good use at a time of cheap stock prices.

With the consensus still estimating rich cash flows and $8.4B still remaining in its repurchase authorization, we believe that the SaaS company remains well positioned to return great value to long-term shareholders before the eventual stock price reversal.

ADBE's Discounted Valuations Trigger Deep Value Buy Opportunity

ADBE Valuations

These reasons are also why we believe that ADBE has been overly discounted at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 17.53x, compared to the 1Y mean of 20.28x, the 5Y mean of 32.08x, and the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 32.34x.

When comparing to the sector median of 1.86x and ADBE's Creative SaaS/ Enterprise SaaS/ AI SaaS peers, including:

  • Autodesk (ADSK) at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 32.67x and 5Y mean of 39.13x,
  • Figma (FIG) at 196.09x/ NA,
  • Microsoft (MSFT) at 32.81x/ 31.90x,
  • Oracle (ORCL) at 46.09x/ 20.69x, and
  • Palantir (PLTR) at 284.13x/ 135.56x, respectively,

it goes without saying that the former is cheaply valued at current levels.

The same has been observed in ADBE's compelling FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.22x as well, compared to the sector median of 1.86x and its peers, including

  • ADSK at 2.15x
  • FIG at 17.82x,
  • MSFT at 2.78x,
  • ORCL at 3.02x, and
  • PLTR at 8.03x,

with it underscoring the former's deep-value investment thesis at a time of dangerous AI bubble, as highlighted by OpenAI's and Meta's (META) top management.

Here is where we believe that opportunistic investors may want to double down on this undervalued stock, since ADBE's robust performance metrics and excellent shareholder returns are simply awaiting one positive catalyst for an upward re-rating, significantly aided by the consistent bounce at its 1Y P/E floor of 17x.

The same has been similarly observed in another underdog, Alphabet (GOOG), with the stock previously deeply discounted at a 3Y P/E mean of ~20x compared to its Magnificent 7 peers at a P/E mean of ~42x between Q2'22 and Q2'25.

Well, this is before the much needed clarity from the recent anti-trust ruling, which triggers the much needed reversal in market sentiments and the notable upgrading in GOOG's FWD P/E to 25x by the time of writing.

These reasons are also why ADBE remains a compelling deep-value play for patient investors, since its upcoming reversal are likely to trigger a rich upgrading cadence and consequently, expanded capital appreciation prospects.

So, Is ADBE Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

ADBE 6Y Stock Price

For now, the bulls have continued to defend ADBE's floor at the $340s ranges, as the stock seemingly consolidates nearer to its 50/ 100 day moving averages.

We maintain our belief that the stock remains cheap against our base-case fair value estimates of $365, based on the management's raised FY2025 adj EPS guidance of $20.825 at the midpoint (+13% YoY) and the current FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 17.53x.

There remains an excellent upside potential of +26.9% to our base-case long-term price target of $459.40 as well, based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $26.21.

Assuming an eventual re-rating nearer to the sector P/E median of 25x (still cheap compared to the 5Y mean and the peer group median of 32x), as discussed in the valuation segment above, ADBE may very well enjoy a rich near doubling upside potential to our bull-case long-term price target of $654 as well.

ADBE's Technical Indicators

Technical indicators also suggest improving trading volumes since the early 2025 bottom, with historical trends already suggesting a potential upward momentum from current bottoms.

Combined with the promising AI monetization trends, the ongoing shareholder returns, the well supported 1Y P/E floor at the 17x, and the established support at the $340s ranges, we are reiterating our Buy rating for the ADBE stock here.

Do not miss this potential long-term AI winner.

data.title: Aon: Almost Time To Take Out Insurance
data.description:
Shares of Aon (NYSE:AON) have been stagnating a bit in recent years despite the continuation of organic growth and M&A driven growth, which makes it so that valuation multiples over time have come down.

This makes me more upbeat from a risk-reward perspective, and amidst a very small premium compared to the market, I am lured to get involved here, although I would like to see a continued dip before pulling the trigger.

Powerful Platform For Growth

AON serves large, attractive, and growing markets, these being combined a more than $4.5 trillion health, commercial risk, and reinsurance market. The company brokers and provides risk management solutions for private equity, sovereign wealth funds, pension funds, and other clients.

The company benefits from strong growth from various trends, including larger losses from natural disasters amidst great occurrence and higher replacement costs, rising healthcare costs, increasing benefits as a percentage of compensation, and growing uncertainty about retirement among consumers.

The company generates about $16 billion in annual sales, of which half are generated from commercial risks, and the remainder from notable health, reinsurance, and wealth solutions. The company is very globally diversified, with half of sales generated in North America, nearly a third generated in the EMEA region, complemented by smaller activities in the wider Asian region and Canada and Latin America.

With customer requirements on the increase, scale is of importance, with AON being strong in data, analytics, innovation, and capital, all of which require scale to deliver on synergies and better insights.

The company has seen solid improvements over the past decade, having grown revenues from less than $10 billion a decade ago to $16 billion by now. Margins have increased, now exceeding a quarter of sales, as the company bought back nearly one in every four shares over the past decade, all resulting in very strong achievements on a per-share basis.

This has been well reflected in the share price, as a $100 stock in 2015 broke the $300 mark in 2021, hit highs just over $400 earlier this year, now trading around $350 per share.

The Base Case

In January of this year, AON reported a 17% increase in 2024 sales to $15.7 billion, with adjusted operating margins being very strong at 31.5% of sales, allowing for a 10% increase in adjusted earnings to $15.60 per share. Organic growth was reported at 6%, with the growth largely being driven by a $13 billion acquisition of NFP, which closed in April 2024. As a result, debt incurred made it so that improvements on the bottom line trailed the top-line sales trends.

Revenues from risk capital came in at $10.5 billion, notably from commercial risk and, to a smaller extent, reinsurance solutions, these showing solid high single digit organic growth rates. Human capital revenues rose by more than 30%, aided by the NFP deal announced in 2023, with total revenues of $5.2 billion now making up a third of total sales.

Based on current price levels, shares trade at 22 times earnings reported for 2024. Given the pace of organic growth and the fact that NFP only starts to contribute in full to the 2025 results, naturally growth was seen in 2025.

Amidst optimism in the business, the company hiked its dividend by 10% in April, now payable at $2.98 per share each year, for a dividend yield still trailing 1%, but this is a nice increase in the dividend nonetheless.

Momentum continued in the first half of the year, with reported sales up 16% and organic revenues up 5%. Second quarter sales were up 11%, amidst a solid 6% increase in organic sales, with adjusted earnings so far this year up sixty-seven cents on the year before, making a $17 per share number likely reasonable for this year.

Net debt is reported at $15.9 billion, yet with adjusted EBIT (this not being a very asset-intensive business) trending around $6 billion, leverage ratios are seen in the higher 2s, perfectly manageable, also considering the defensive nature of the operations.

With 217 million shares trading around $350 per share, the equity valuation of the firm stands at $76 billion, for a $92 billion enterprise valuation. This values the business at just over 5 times sales, seen exceeding $17 billion this year.

Leveraging Down

Early in September, AON announced that it had reached a deal to sell some of NFP's wealth business, including Wealthspire Advisors, Fiducient Advisors, Newport Private Wealth, to its former owners, Madison Dearborn Partners. The activities are sold in a $2.7 billion deal, with net cash proceeds seen at $2.2 billion, indicating that this is somewhat of a bolt-on sale.

With the business generating about $127 million in EBITDA, the multiple fetched comes in at around 21 times EBITDA ahead of taxes, and 17 times EBITDA based on the net cash proceeds, which frankly looks favorable with the own business trading around 15 times EBITDA. This furthermore allows for some net debt reduction, with pro forma net debt seen below $14 billion, reducing leverage towards, or just below, 2.5 times EBITDA here.

Reaching Fair Valuations

With earnings easily seen around $16-17 per share, earnings multiples have come down to 20-22 times earnings, as shares have seen long term returns, but frankly trade at levels seen early in 2022 already, marking some underperformance in recent years.

This has resulted in valuation multiple deflation, with these multiples starting to look more compelling, as I like the combination of organic growth, M&A and buybacks, with the latest divestment looking to take place at fair valuations, reducing leverage, which is very acceptable.

This results in the relative appeal rapidly increasing, as I think that a small premium to the market is warranted given the strong track record, as current levels start to look compelling, making me interested to get involved on further dips in the lower $300s.
